### Post by spadewarrior on 2009-01-04
Hi all.

I've just put an 80gb drive from an old computer into my 8.10 install. This is my third hard drive(!) in this computer.

The problem is that whilst I can create/delete files, only about 700mb is available on the volume when there should be about 60+gb...

Any ideas? Here is my fstab

```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=e01e59d6-d224-4dcd-9cbf-7348daac5b1b / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda3 :
UUID=f82a22d4-066f-48e9-9fdf-004984658ac6 /home ext3 relatime 0 2
# Entry for /dev/sdb1 :
UUID=47CF-E034 /windows vfat utf8,umask=007,gid=46 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=4fd8193e-49e3-40a1-b582-0675dde0f6e6 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 
# Third Hard drive /dev/sdb5 (80gb) :
/dev/sdb5 /third ext2 exec,rw,user,umask=007 0 0
```

---

```

--cut--

/dev/sdb5 /third ext2 exec,rw,user,umask=007 0 0

```

df -h /dev/sdb5
sudo fdisk -l /dev/sdb

```

udev                  505M  2.8M  502M   1% /dev

```


```

~$ sudo fdisk -l /dev/sdb

Disk /dev/sdb: 81.9 GB, 81964302336 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 9964 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

### Post by albinootje on 2009-01-04
> **spadewarrior said:**
> 
```

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sdb1               1        9964    80035798+   5  Extended
/dev/sdb5               1        9964    80035767   83  Linux

```
Weird.

Assuming that you have made proper regular backups already..

Can you remove /dev/sdb1 and /dev/sdb5 and create a new primary partition on /dev/sdb1 and format that ?

-> System -> Administration -> Partition Editor
```

sudo apt-get install gparted 

```
if it's not installed.

### Post by spadewarrior on 2009-01-04
Hmm, looks like I didn't know what I was doing with gparted when I first put the drive in...

I'll give that a go. Thanks :).

### Post by taurus on 2009-01-04
Make sure you unmount /dev/sdb5 first before you fire up gparted or you would see a "key" (locked) to /dev/sdb1.

```
sudo umount /dev/sdb5
```

And if you plan to recreate a single primary partition, /dev/sdb1, I would mount it in /etc/fstab as

```
/dev/sdb1   /third   ext2   **defaults**   0   2
```

---

### Post by spadewarrior on 2009-01-04
OK, I've added that line to my fstab and commented-out the old one.

Nautilus now shows the correct amount of space left but all I can see is 'lost and found'... What can I add to make this hard drive accessible to me everytime I boot up? 

Here are the new outputs from those commands, in case you want to see them:

```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0

### Post by taurus on 2009-01-04
Well, you just have to change the ownership of /third from root to your login name so you can write to it anytime you want.  _Assuming_ your login name is spade, do

```
sudo chown -R spade:spade /third
touch /third/testing
ls -la /third
```

You should see a file called testing in /third directory with 0 byte.  To remove it, run

```
rm /third/testing
ls -la /third
```

---

### Post by albinootje on 2009-01-04
> **spadewarrior said:**
> OK, I've added that line to my fstab and commented-out the old one.

Nautilus now shows the correct amount of space left but all I can see is 'lost and found'... What can I add to make this hard drive accessible to me everytime I boot up? 


This is good.
And lost+found is a normal directory on ext2 or ext3 partitions.
If you want your normal user to read+write on it, do e.g. the following :
```

sudo mkdir /third/data-dir
sudo chown 1000:1000 /third/data-dir

```
Assuming that you're using the username that was created during the Ubuntu installation.

---

### Post by spadewarrior on 2009-01-04
Thanks to you both. That seems to have solved the issue :). Now to get on with all the backing-up I've been meaning to do...
